experiences.intentions: Key state: 4x4 2048 board with two empties at top-left positions, highest tile 128 at row 3 col 2. Goal is to create merges while preserving board flexibility and avoiding filling the board with no move quality. Candidate actions: Left makes immediate merges in row 2 (4+4) and row 1 shifts 4 and 8 left, increasing empties and improving structure; Right also merges row 2 but pushes high tiles away from left-side buildup and is less favorable. Up compresses columns with no merges and reduces empties. Down also gives no merges and can clutter the bottom. Best sub-goal now is gain space and secure an immediate merge, so choose left.

experiences.intentions: Key state: highest tile 128 is at row 3 col 2, with only two empty spaces at top row col 3 and second row col 4. We want to preserve mobility and create merges without disrupting the strong lower rows. Evaluating moves: left compresses top rows but creates no merges and fills one space; right also mostly shifts tiles without merges and risks scattering. Down appears invalid/full with no useful compression because columns are already packed toward bottom. Up is strongest: column 4 has [2,0,4,8] -> [2,4,8,0], creating an empty cell; other columns stay stable, and crucially column 3 has [0,4,8,32] -> [4,8,32,0], preserving order while increasing empty spaces to improve survival. Sub-goal now is maximizing open spaces while keeping large tiles anchored. इसलिए up is best.

experiences.intentions: Board is nearly full with only one empty space at bottom-right. The grid is already monotonic upward in value toward the lower middle, and preserving empty spaces is critical. Up and down appear to cause no merges because columns have no adjacent equal values; they likely either do nothing or worsen structure. Right would move the bottom row [4,8,2,0] to [0,4,8,2], creating an empty corner on the left but breaking stability and shifting tiles away from the current anchored structure. Left moves the bottom row into [4,8,2,0], effectively preserving the existing empty bottom-right corner while keeping the board as stable as possible. Goal is survival and maintaining the empty corner/subsequent spawn space without disrupting the high-value layout. Therefore left is safest.

experiences.intentions: The board is completely full, so only moves that create merges are legal/useful. Checking rows and columns: left/right produce no horizontal merges on any row. Up/down can merge in column 3 (4,8,32,8 no), column 4 (2,16,4,4 gives a merge only at the bottom if moved down/up? adjacent equals only bottom two 4s in col 4), and especially column 1 has 4,8,32,2 no; column 2 has 8,16,128,4 no. In column 4, moving down merges the two 4s at the bottom into 8 while keeping larger tiles lower, and moving up also merges but tends to place smaller resulting tiles higher. Goal is to preserve board mobility and keep larger tiles anchored; down is the best sub-goal action.

experiences.intentions: Board is full, so only moves that create merges are legal/useful. Rows have a merge only on the bottom row to the right/left (8,8), and the last column has a vertical merge at the top two 2s, so up/down and left/right are candidates. Goal is to preserve structure around the 128 and avoid disrupting the stronger monotonic middle columns while still making a legal merge. Moving right merges the bottom-row 8s into a 16 at the far right and keeps the 128 fixed in place; left would pull that row away from the right edge, and up/down would disturb multiple columns more. So right best maintains board organization while opening one space.

experiences.intentions: This is a 2048 board with no empty spaces, so only moves that create merges are legal/useful. Bottom row has [2,2,4,16], giving an immediate merge if moved left or right. Checking full-board effects: left yields rows [4,8,4,2], [8,16,8,2], [32,128,32,16], [4,4,16,0], creating one merge and opening a space while keeping the 128 anchored. Right similarly merges the bottom 2s but places the gap on the left. Up/down produce no merges in any column and likely do nothing. Goal is to survive and preserve structure around the 128; left is slightly better because it keeps larger tiles more consolidated toward the left and creates an empty space on the right edge.

experiences.intentions: Board is full, so only moves that create merges are legal/progressive. Current rows/cols show immediate merges on the bottom row (4,4) with left/right, and in the last column (2,2) at top with down/up-adjacent arrangement allowing compression. Goal is to preserve the high 128 while creating space and maintaining structure. Up would merge the two 2s in the last column into a 4 while keeping the 128 in place and preserving the stronger larger tiles clustered lower/middle. Down would also merge but drags small tiles downward and disrupts the bottom row. Left/right merge the bottom 4s but shift many rows more aggressively and risk worse ordering around the 128. Best sub-goal is create one empty space with minimal disruption, so choose up.

experiences.intentions: The board is full, so I need a move that creates at least one merge; otherwise the game ends. Horizontal moves produce no merges in any row. Vertical moves do: in column 4 the two 2 tiles at the bottom can merge with either up or down, and in column 1 the two 4 tiles can also merge with down after compression. My goal is to preserve the high 128 tile while maximizing future space. Moving down creates two merges (col 1: 4+4, col 4: 2+2), yielding more empty space than up, so down is the best sub-goal for survival and board flexibility. Up only merges the 2s in column 4. Left and right appear invalid with no merges.
